schools.summary: The strange fire of Nadab and Abihu: 'and Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, took either of them his censer, and put fire therein, and put incense thereon, and offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not' — the strange fire — 'offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not': Nadab and Abihu (Aaron's two eldest sons) taking their censers, putting fire and incense, and offering 'strange fire' before the LORD, fire he had not commanded (the witnesses noting the significance — the strange fire, common fire, not from the altar's holy fire kindled from heaven, Leviticus 9:24; 6:13, or incense offered at a wrong time or way, in any case unauthorized, 'which he commanded them not,' the lesson — the sin of will-worship and unauthorized worship, the offering of what God had not commanded, presuming to worship God in their own way and not his, the great sin of adding to or altering God's appointed worship, 'in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men,' Matthew 15:9; Colossians 2:23, will-worship, the gravity, the worship not commanded is forbidden, 'which he commanded them not' — God's silence not license but prohibition in worship, the regulative principle, Deuteronomy 12:32, 'what thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it,' the figure — the warning against all will-worship and human invention in the worship of God, some adding the likelihood they were drunk, vv.9, the prohibition of wine following); the censers, the fire, the incense (the act of presumption, the unauthorized offering) — the presumption (the priests acting of their own will, not by command, the figure, the will-worship, the human addition); strange fire, which he commanded them not — the worship not commanded (the providence, the sin of unauthorized worship, the judgment to follow, vv.2) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What was the sin of Nadab and Abihu?

sides.position: The sin of will-worship and unauthorized worship - the offering of what God had not commanded, worshipping God in man's way and not his (Matthew 15:9; Colossians 2:23), God's silence in worship a prohibition not a license (Deuteronomy 12:32, neither adding nor diminishing), the warning against all human invention in worship

themes: And Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron... offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not
the strange fire of Nadab and Abihu (the crux of unauthorized worship)
the sin of will-worship, worshipping God in man's way and not his (Matthew 15:9; Colossians 2:23)
God's silence in worship a prohibition, the warning against human invention (Deuteronomy 12:32)

schools.summary: The fire of judgment devouring Nadab and Abihu: 'and there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them, and they died before the LORD' — the fire — 'there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them': the same fire that had just consumed the offering in acceptance (Leviticus 9:24) now going out from the LORD and devouring Nadab and Abihu, and they dying (the witnesses noting the significance — the swift, severe judgment on the unauthorized worship, the fire of God's holiness, which accepted the right offering, now destroying the false, the same fire of God a savour of life to the obedient and of death to the presumptuous, the lesson — the severity of God's holiness against the profaning of his worship, the awful judgment, the warning that God will not be mocked nor worshipped in man's way, the fire that sanctifies and the fire that consumes, Hebrews 12:29, 'our God is a consuming fire,' the gravity, the judgment swift, public, at the very inauguration of the priesthood, a warning to all, the figure — the holiness of God that consumes the profane, the seriousness of approaching God wrongly, the warning of 1 Corinthians 11:30, the judgment on unworthy approach, the fear of God in worship); the death of Nadab and Abihu before the LORD (the swift judgment, the bodies consumed yet the garments perhaps untouched, vv.5) — the severity (the judgment on the profane, the figure, the death the wages of presuming on God's holiness) — Wesley, JFB, Gill.

themes: And there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them, and they died before the LORD
the fire of judgment devouring Nadab and Abihu
the same fire that accepted the offering now consuming the presumptuous (Leviticus 9:24)
the severity of God's holiness against the profaning of his worship (Hebrews 12:29)

schools.summary: God sanctified in them that come nigh, and Aaron's silence: 'then Moses said unto Aaron, This is it that the LORD spake, saying, I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and before all the people I will be glorified. And Aaron held his peace' — the sanctifying — 'I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and before all the people I will be glorified': Moses explaining the judgment by God's word, that he would be sanctified (treated as holy) in those who draw near to him (the priests), and glorified before all the people (the witnesses noting the significance — God's holiness vindicated, the more privileged the nearness, the greater the holiness required, those who come nigh to God, especially the priests, must treat him as holy, or he will vindicate his holiness in judgment, the lesson — God will be sanctified, one way or another, either by the reverent obedience of those who draw near, or by judgment on their profaneness, 'be not deceived; God is not mocked,' Galatians 6:7, the nearer to God, the holier the requirement, 'judgment must begin at the house of God,' 1 Peter 4:17, the glorifying, God glorified before the people by the judgment, his holiness made known, the figure — the holiness of God that must be honored, the reverence due in worship and in those who minister); 'And Aaron held his peace' (was silent) — the silence of submission (Aaron, the bereaved father, silent in submission to God's righteous judgment, neither murmuring nor complaining, accepting the justice of God, like Job, 'the LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD,' Job 1:21; Psalm 39:9, 'I was dumb, I opened not my mouth; because thou didst it,' the figure, the silent submission to God's holy will, the resignation of faith) — Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me' teach?

sides.position: God's holiness must be honored, especially by those who draw near (the priests) - he will be sanctified either by their reverence or by judgment on their profaneness (1 Peter 4:17), the nearer to God the holier the requirement; and Aaron's silence the model of submission to God's righteous judgment (Job 1:21; Psalm 39:9)

themes: I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and before all the people I will be glorified. And Aaron held his peace
God sanctified in them that come nigh, and Aaron's silence (the crux of God's holiness vindicated)
God's holiness honored by those who draw near, or vindicated in judgment (1 Peter 4:17)
Aaron's silence the model of submission to God's righteous judgment (Job 1:21; Psalm 39:9)

schools.summary: The cousins carrying out the bodies: 'and Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel the uncle of Aaron, and said unto them, Come near, carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp' — Moses calling the cousins of Nadab and Abihu (sons of Uzziel, Aaron's uncle) to carry the bodies out of the camp (the cousins, the kinsmen, not the priests, removing the dead, the carrying out, the bodies removed from the sanctuary, the dead not to defile the holy place, the out of the camp, the bodies taken outside, the place of the dead, the figure, the removal of the dead from the sanctuary, the holiness guarded, the priests not to defile themselves, vv.6-7); the cousins called (not the priests, who were not to touch the dead, vv.6) — the removal (the kinsmen carrying out the bodies); 'carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp' (the bodies removed from the sanctuary, the holiness guarded, the dead not to defile the holy place, the figure, the holiness of the sanctuary, the dead removed, the providence, the bodies carried out, the priests kept from defilement, vv.6-7) — Clarke, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel the uncle of Aaron... carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp
the cousins carrying out the bodies
the kinsmen removing the dead from the sanctuary, not the priests
the holiness of the holy place guarded (vv.6-7)

schools.summary: The bodies carried out in their coats: 'so they went near, and carried them in their coats out of the camp; as Moses had said' — the cousins carrying the bodies out in their coats (the priestly tunics), as Moses said (the in their coats, the bodies still in their priestly garments, the fire having consumed their lives but not their clothes, the carried out, removed from the camp, the as Moses said, the obedience, the figure, the fire devouring the men but not their garments, the strange and awful judgment, the bodies removed in their coats); the bodies in their coats (the priestly tunics, the fire having taken their lives but not their clothes, the awful judgment) — the wonder (the fire consuming the men, not the garments); 'out of the camp; as Moses had said' (the bodies removed as commanded, the obedience, the figure, the holiness guarded, the providence, the bodies carried out, the judgment's marks) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: So they went near, and carried them in their coats out of the camp; as Moses had said
the bodies carried out in their coats
the bodies still in their priestly garments, the fire consuming the men but not their coats
the awful judgment, removed as Moses said

schools.summary: The priests forbidden to mourn for the slain: 'and Moses said unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons, Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes; lest ye die, and lest wrath come upon all the people: but let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning which the LORD hath kindled' — Aaron and his surviving sons forbidden to show the usual signs of mourning (uncovering the head, rending the clothes) for Nadab and Abihu, on pain of death (the prohibition of mourning, the priests not to mourn in the customary way, the reason, the priests consecrated, in the midst of their ministry, not to defile their consecration with mourning, or to seem to dispute God's righteous judgment, the lest ye die, the severity, the priests to submit, not to mourn the justly judged); the priests not to mourn (lest they die, lest wrath come on the people) — the lesson (the priests to submit to God's judgment, not to dispute it by mourning, the consecration not to be interrupted, the acknowledgment of the justice of God's judgment, even by the bereaved); 'let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning which the LORD hath kindled' (the people, not the priests, might lament the judgment, the public grief permitted to the congregation, the figure, the priests submitting, the people mourning, the providence, the priests in their ministry, the people bewailing) — Clarke, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

themes: Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes; lest ye die... but let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning which the LORD hath kindled
the priests forbidden to mourn for the slain
the priests not to interrupt their consecration nor dispute God's righteous judgment
submission to God's holy judgment even in bereavement, while the people bewail

schools.summary: The priests kept at the sanctuary by the anointing: 'and ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses' — the priests not to leave the door of the tabernacle (to follow the dead, or mourn), lest they die, because the anointing oil of the LORD was upon them (the not going out, the priests to remain at their ministry, not to leave for the funeral, the the anointing oil upon them, the consecration, the priests set apart, not to be interrupted by mourning, the lest ye die, the severity, the consecration to be honored, the figure, the consecration of the priests, the claim of God's service above natural ties, the obedience of the priests); the priests kept at the sanctuary (not leaving for the dead, the consecration, vv.6) — the consecration (the priests set apart for God's service); 'for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses' (the anointing oil, the consecration, the claim of God's service, the obedience, the priests doing as Moses said, submitting, the figure, the consecration above natural ties, the obedience of submission, Matthew 8:22, 'let the dead bury their dead; but go thou and preach,' the providence, the priests at their ministry, the obedience) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses
the priests kept at the sanctuary by the anointing
the priests kept at their ministry by their consecration, not to leave for the dead
the claim of God's service above natural ties, the obedience of submission (Matthew 8:22)

schools.summary: The LORD speaking directly to Aaron concerning wine: 'and the LORD spake unto Aaron, saying' — the LORD speaking directly to Aaron (a rare honor, usually through Moses), now to give the prohibition of wine for the priests in their ministry (vv.9-11, the speaking to Aaron, the LORD addressing Aaron directly, perhaps to comfort and honor him after his loss and submission, the occasion, after the death of his sons, the prohibition of wine, perhaps suggesting Nadab and Abihu had been drunk, the figure, God's direct word to Aaron, the honor and the instruction, God's tender regard for the submissive, honoring the bereaved father with his own word); the LORD speaking directly to Aaron (a mark of honor, usually through Moses) — the honor (God addressing Aaron, perhaps comforting and honoring him after his submission); the LORD spake unto Aaron — the prohibition of wine introduced (the providence, God instructing Aaron, the sober priesthood, vv.9-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Aaron, saying
the LORD speaking directly to Aaron concerning wine
God's direct word to Aaron, a rare honor, after his loss and submission
the prohibition of wine for the sober priesthood (vv.9-11)

schools.summary: The prohibition of wine for the priests in their ministry: 'do not drink wine nor strong drink, thou, nor thy sons with thee, when ye go into the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: it shall be a statute for ever throughout your generations' — the priests forbidden to drink wine or strong drink when they went in to minister, on pain of death, a perpetual statute (the prohibition, no wine or strong drink in the ministry, the lest ye die, the severity, the sobriety required, the perpetual statute, the standing rule for the priests, the figure, the sobriety of those who minister, the clear mind required in God's service, perhaps Nadab and Abihu had been drunk, the connection with their sin); the priests to be sober in their ministry (no wine, the clear mind, the reverence) — the lesson (the sobriety required in God's service, the danger of drunkenness in worship, the warning, perhaps from Nadab and Abihu's sin, the figure, the sober, watchful service, 1 Peter 5:8, the minister sober, 1 Timothy 3:2-3); 'it shall be a statute for ever throughout your generations' (the perpetual statute, the standing rule, the figure, the sobriety of God's servants, the clear mind in worship, the providence, the sober priesthood, the perpetual rule) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

themes: Do not drink wine nor strong drink, thou, nor thy sons with thee, when ye go into the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: it shall be a statute for ever
the prohibition of wine for the priests in their ministry
the priests to be sober in God's service, the clear mind and reverence required
a figure of the sobriety and watchfulness required of God's servants (1 Peter 5:8; 1 Timothy 3:2-3)

schools.summary: The priest's task of distinguishing holy from unholy: 'and that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean' — the discernment — 'that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean': one reason for the sobriety (vv.9) being that the priests might discern between holy and unholy, clean and unclean (the witnesses noting the significance — the priest's task of discernment, the priests to distinguish the holy from the common, the clean from the unclean, to judge and teach the difference, which a drunken mind could not do, the lesson — the discernment required in God's service, the clear mind to distinguish the holy from the profane, the office of the priest to discern and teach the difference, Ezekiel 44:23, 'they shall teach my people the difference between the holy and profane, and cause them to discern between the unclean and the clean,' the figure — the spiritual discernment of God's people, the believer to discern, to try the spirits, to distinguish good and evil, Hebrews 5:14, 'to discern both good and evil'; 1 Thessalonians 5:21, 'prove all things; hold fast that which is good,' the sober, discerning mind required in the things of God); the distinguishing of holy and unholy (the priest's discernment of the sacred) — the discernment (the holy distinguished from the common); 'between unclean and clean' (the distinguishing of clean and unclean, the ceremonial discernment, the laws of clean and unclean, chs. 11-15, the figure, the discernment of the holy and the clean, the providence, the priest's task to discern and teach, vv.11, the sober mind for discernment) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the priest's task of discernment teach?

sides.position: The priest's task to distinguish the holy from the common and teach the difference (Ezekiel 44:23), requiring the sober, clear mind (vv.9) - a figure of the spiritual discernment required of God's people (Hebrews 5:14; 1 Thessalonians 5:21), the trying of all things and distinguishing of good and evil

themes: And that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean
the priest's task of distinguishing holy from unholy (the crux of discernment)
the priest to distinguish the holy from the common and teach the difference (Ezekiel 44:23)
a figure of the spiritual discernment required of God's people (Hebrews 5:14; 1 Thessalonians 5:21)

schools.summary: The priest's task of teaching the statutes: 'and that ye may teach the children of Israel all the statutes which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses' — another task of the priests (requiring the sober mind, vv.9) being to teach Israel all the statutes God had given by Moses (the teaching, the priests to instruct the people in God's law, the all the statutes, the whole law, the ordinances, the by the hand of Moses, the law given through Moses, the figure, the priest's office to teach, the ministry of the word, Malachi 2:7, 'the priest's lips should keep knowledge, and they should seek the law at his mouth'); the priests to teach the law (the office of instruction, Deuteronomy 33:10) — the figure (the priest the teacher, the ministry of the word, Malachi 2:7); 'which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses' (the law given through Moses, the whole law to be taught, the figure, the ministry of the word, the teaching office, the providence, the priests teaching the law, the sober mind required, vv.9) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And that ye may teach the children of Israel all the statutes which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses
the priest's task of teaching the statutes
the priests to teach the people God's law, the ministry of the word (Malachi 2:7)
the priest's lips keeping knowledge (Deuteronomy 33:10)

schools.summary: Moses commanding the priests to eat the meal offering: 'and Moses spake unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons that were left, Take the meat offering that remaineth of the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and eat it without leaven beside the altar: for it is most holy' — Moses commanding Aaron and his surviving sons to eat the remainder of the meal offering, unleavened, beside the altar, for it was most holy (the sons that were left, Eleazar and Ithamar, the survivors after the death of Nadab and Abihu, the eating the meal offering, the priests' portion, unleavened, Leviticus 6:16, the most holy, the highest holiness, the figure, the priests fed from the offering even in the day of judgment, the ministry to continue); the priests eating the meal offering (the priests' portion, unleavened, Leviticus 6:16) — the provision (the priests fed from the offering, the ministry sustained even amid the judgment); 'for it is most holy' (the most holy, the highest holiness, the priests' sacred portion, the figure, the holy food, the participation in the offering, the providence, the priests eating the most holy offering, the ministry continuing) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And Moses spake unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons that were left, Take the meat offering that remaineth... and eat it without leaven beside the altar: for it is most holy
Moses commanding the priests to eat the meal offering
the surviving priests fed from the most holy offering (Leviticus 6:16)
the ministry continuing even in the day of judgment

schools.summary: The meal offering eaten in the holy place as the priests' due: 'and ye shall eat it in the holy place, because it is thy due, and thy sons' due, of the sacrifices of the LORD made by fire: for so I am commanded' — the meal offering to be eaten in the holy place, as the priests' due (their appointed portion), as Moses was commanded (the holy place, the sacred eating, the the priests' due, their appointed portion, Leviticus 6:16, the for so I am commanded, the command of God, Moses declaring it, the figure, the priests' portion, the ministry's living, the appointed due); the meal offering the priests' due (their appointed portion, Leviticus 6:16) — the provision (the priests' due, the ministry sustained); 'for so I am commanded' (the command of God, Moses declaring the divine appointment, the figure, the appointed portion by God's command, the providence, the priests' due by command, the ministry maintained) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And ye shall eat it in the holy place, because it is thy due, and thy sons' due, of the sacrifices of the LORD made by fire: for so I am commanded
the meal offering eaten in the holy place as the priests' due
the priests' appointed portion by God's command (Leviticus 6:16)
the ministry sustained

schools.summary: The wave breast and heave shoulder eaten as the priests' due: 'and the wave breast and heave shoulder shall ye eat in a clean place; thou, and thy sons, and thy daughters with thee: for they be thy due, and thy sons' due, which are given out of the sacrifices of peace offerings of the children of Israel' — the wave breast and heave shoulder of the peace offerings to be eaten in a clean place by the priests and their families, as their due (the wave breast and heave shoulder, the priests' portion of the peace offerings, Leviticus 7:31-34, the in a clean place, not necessarily the holy place, but any clean place, the less holy portion, the the family eating, the priests' sons and daughters, the whole household, sharing the peace offering portion, the figure, the priests' portion, the fellowship of the peace offering shared by the household); the breast and shoulder eaten in a clean place (the priests' portion of the peace offerings, Leviticus 7:31-34) — the portion (the priests' due); 'thou, and thy sons, and thy daughters with thee: for they be thy due' (the whole household eating, the priests' family sharing, unlike the most holy offerings, the figure, the fellowship of the peace offering shared by the household, the providence, the priests' due, the family fed) — JFB, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the wave breast and heave shoulder shall ye eat in a clean place; thou, and thy sons, and thy daughters with thee: for they be thy due
the wave breast and heave shoulder eaten as the priests' due
the priests' portion of the peace offerings shared by the family (Leviticus 7:31-34)
the fellowship of the peace offering shared by the household

schools.summary: The wave breast and heave shoulder a perpetual due: 'the heave shoulder and the wave breast shall they bring with the offerings made by fire of the fat, to wave it for a wave offering before the LORD; and it shall be thine, and thy sons' with thee, by a statute for ever; as the LORD hath commanded' — the heave shoulder and wave breast, brought with the fat and waved before the LORD, the priests' perpetual due (the waving, dedicated to God by the waving, then the priests', the by a statute for ever, the perpetual due, the as the LORD commanded, the divine appointment, Leviticus 7:34, the figure, the priests' perpetual portion, God's provision for the ministry); the breast and shoulder waved (dedicated to God, then the priests', Leviticus 7:30) — the dedication (the portion dedicated and given); 'by a statute for ever; as the LORD hath commanded' (the perpetual statute, the priests' due for ever, Leviticus 7:34, the figure, God's perpetual provision for the ministry, the providence, the priests' portion perpetual, the ministry maintained) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: The heave shoulder and the wave breast shall they bring with the offerings made by fire of the fat, to wave it for a wave offering before the LORD... by a statute for ever; as the LORD hath commanded
the wave breast and heave shoulder a perpetual due
the priests' perpetual portion, God's provision for the ministry (Leviticus 7:34)
dedicated to God by the waving, then given to the priests by his command

schools.summary: Moses' anger that the sin offering was burnt: 'and Moses diligently sought the goat of the sin offering, and, behold, it was burnt: and he was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, the sons of Aaron which were left alive, saying' — Moses seeking the goat of the people's sin offering, finding it had been burnt (not eaten, as the law required when the blood was not brought into the holy place, Leviticus 6:26), and being angry with the surviving priests (the diligent seeking, Moses' careful inquiry into the right doing of the service, the it was burnt, the sin offering burnt instead of eaten, contrary to the law, the anger, Moses' zeal for the right observance, even amid the day of grief); Moses' diligent inquiry (the careful attention to the right doing of the service, even in the day of judgment) — the zeal (Moses' care for the exact observance, the seriousness of the service); 'he was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, the sons of Aaron which were left alive' (Moses' anger at the apparent breach, the sin offering not eaten as commanded, the figure, the zeal for the right observance, even from grief, the providence, Moses' rebuke, Aaron's answer to follow, vv.19) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

themes: And Moses diligently sought the goat of the sin offering, and, behold, it was burnt: and he was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, the sons of Aaron which were left alive
Moses' anger that the sin offering was burnt
Moses' diligent inquiry into the right doing of the service, even amid grief
the sin offering to be eaten, not burnt (Leviticus 6:26)

schools.summary: The sin offering eaten to bear the iniquity of the congregation: 'wherefore have ye not eaten the sin offering in the holy place, seeing it is most holy, and God hath given it you to bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them before the LORD?' — the bearing of iniquity — 'God hath given it you to bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them before the LORD': Moses rebuking the priests for not eating the sin offering, which God had given them to bear the iniquity of the people and make atonement (the witnesses noting the significance — the priest, by eating the sin offering, bearing (symbolically) the iniquity of the people, sharing in the atonement, the eating part of the atoning ministry, the priest taking the people's sin upon himself in figure, the lesson — the priest's participation in the atonement, the eating of the sin offering a sacred act of the atoning ministry, the priest bearing the iniquity, not a mere meal, the figure — Christ the great High Priest who truly bore the iniquity of his people, Isaiah 53:11, 'he shall bear their iniquities'; John 1:29, 'the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world'; Hebrews 9:28, 'Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many,' the priest's eating a figure of the bearing of sin, fulfilled in Christ who bore our sins in his own body, the seriousness of the priestly office); the sin offering most holy, to be eaten in the holy place (the sacred portion, Leviticus 6:25-26) — the holiness (the sin offering sacred, the eating a holy act, the rebuke, the priests failing to eat as commanded) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the priest's eating of the sin offering signify?

sides.position: The priest, by eating the sin offering, bore (in figure) the iniquity of the people and shared in the atonement (the eating part of the atoning ministry, not a mere meal) - a figure of Christ the great High Priest who truly bore the iniquity of his people (Isaiah 53:11; John 1:29; Hebrews 9:28)

themes: Wherefore have ye not eaten the sin offering in the holy place, seeing it is most holy, and God hath given it you to bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them before the LORD?
the sin offering eaten to bear the iniquity of the congregation
the priest, by eating, bearing the people's iniquity in figure and sharing in the atonement
a figure of Christ who truly bore the iniquity of his people (Isaiah 53:11; John 1:29; Hebrews 9:28)

schools.summary: That the blood not brought in required the eating of the sin offering: 'behold, the blood of it was not brought in within the holy place: ye should indeed have eaten it in the holy place, as I commanded' — Moses pointing out that the blood of this sin offering had not been brought into the holy place (it was a sin offering for the people, the blood applied at the outer altar, Leviticus 4:25-26), so the priests should have eaten it, not burnt it (the rule: sin offerings whose blood was not brought within were eaten, Leviticus 6:30, the blood not brought in, the distinction, Leviticus 6:30, the should have eaten it, the rule for such offerings, the figure, the right observance, the priest bearing the iniquity by eating); the blood not brought into the holy place (the sin offering to be eaten, not burnt, Leviticus 6:30) — the rule (the distinction of the sin offerings); 'ye should indeed have eaten it in the holy place, as I commanded' (the eating commanded, the priests to eat the sin offering, Leviticus 6:26, the figure, the right observance, the bearing of iniquity, the providence, the rule for the sin offering, the priests' duty) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Behold, the blood of it was not brought in within the holy place: ye should indeed have eaten it in the holy place, as I commanded
that the blood not brought in required the eating of the sin offering
the sin offering whose blood was not brought within to be eaten, not burnt (Leviticus 6:26, 30)
the right observance and the bearing of iniquity

schools.summary: Aaron's answer of grief and reverence: 'and Aaron said unto Moses, Behold, this day have they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD; and such things have befallen me: and if I had eaten the sin offering to day, should it have been accepted in the sight of the LORD?' — Aaron answering that, on this day of great affliction (the death of his sons), it would not have been fitting or acceptable for him to eat the sin offering in the festal joy of the priestly meal (the such things have befallen me, the death of Nadab and Abihu, the day of mourning, the should it have been accepted, the question, whether God would have accepted the priestly eating amid such grief, the heart not fit for the festal meal, the answer, Aaron's grief and reverence, not a defiant breach but a heart too stricken to eat the holy meal in joy); Aaron's affliction (the death of his sons, the day of mourning) — the grief (the stricken heart, not fit for the festal eating); 'should it have been accepted in the sight of the LORD?' (Aaron's reverent question, whether God would accept the priestly meal amid such grief, the heart not in it, the figure, the reverence that considers what is acceptable to God, not mere outward observance, the broken heart, Psalm 51:17, the providence, Aaron's answer, accepted by Moses, vv.20) — Clarke, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Aaron said unto Moses, Behold, this day have they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD; and such things have befallen me: and if I had eaten the sin offering to day, should it have been accepted in the sight of the LORD?
Aaron's answer of grief and reverence
Aaron's stricken heart not fit for the festal eating on the day his sons died
the reverence that considers what is acceptable to God, the contrite heart (Psalm 51:17)

schools.summary: Moses content with Aaron's answer: 'and when Moses heard that, he was content' — when Moses heard Aaron's answer, he was satisfied (content, pleased), accepting the reason (the hearing, Moses receiving Aaron's explanation, the content, Moses satisfied, his anger appeased, the answer accepted as good, the figure, the meekness of Moses, accepting a good reason, not insisting rigidly, the wisdom that distinguishes the spirit from the letter, the mercy that considers the grief, the reconciliation of zeal and compassion); Moses hearing Aaron's answer (receiving the explanation) — the receiving (Moses considering the reason); 'he was content' (Moses satisfied, his anger appeased, the answer accepted, the figure, the meekness of Moses, the wisdom that distinguishes the heart from the rigid letter, the mercy that considers grief, Numbers 12:3, the reconciliation of zeal for God's law with compassion, the providence, Moses content, the matter resolved, the chapter closing in peace) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And when Moses heard that, he was content
Moses content with Aaron's answer
Moses satisfied with Aaron's reason, the meekness that accepts a good answer (Numbers 12:3)
the wisdom that distinguishes the heart from the rigid letter and considers the grief
